generalities.text: Main THE ENCYCLOPEDIA OF PURE MATERIA MEDICA By TIMOTHY F. ALLEN, A.M., M.D. Presented by Médi-T <<<<< CLEMATIS. MOUTH. ► Teeth. ► A hollow tooth seems longer than usual, and is painful to the slightest touch, with running of much water from the mouth, [28] . ► Sensation as though the two teeth which had ached the previous night were too long; the gum was less swollen and pale; the pain extended on both sides to the incisors, and made chewing impossible (fourth day), [12] . ► The toothache returned after reading half an hour in bed, not only in a carious hollow tooth of the right side, but also in one on the left side, not relieved by cold water; he fell asleep only towards 1 o'clock; at 10 P.M. (third day), [12] . [190.] ► Toothache in a last lower back tooth, which pained in connection with the upper teeth, even if they were without pain, very much aggravated by getting bread into it , [8] . ► *Toothache, tolerable during the day, but as soon as he lies down in bed and assumes a horizontal position it increases to an unbearable degree , and is not relieved by any position, it only gets better by lying perfectly quiet and enduring the pain for several hours, [8] . ► Toothache which drives him to despair, with tossing about in bed; weakness in the limbs and anxious sweat, so that he cannot endure the covering, lasting the whole night, [8] . ► The toothache extends over the whole temporal region, as far as the vertex, [8] . ► Toothache unfits him for every work, especially for thinking, [8] . ► Dull toothache in hollow tooth, [30] . ► *Dull toothache in a hollow tooth, only transiently relieved by cold water, also relieved by drawing in the air , which causes a stitch as if something pressed upward into the teeth, [8] . ► Sensation in the last upper molar (which was carious), as if it would ache (ninth day), [30] . ► Drawing pains in the teeth, in the nasal bones, in the small of the back, in the toes, in the tibiæ; in each place they are only momentary and of variable intensity, [35] . ► Jerking drawing toothache, during the day, aggravated by smoking tobacco, relieved for only a minute by firm pressure with a cloth, [8] . [200.] ► Jerking sticking-drawing toothache in the left upper jaw, now in one, now in another tooth, often it is uncertain in which tooth of the whole row the pains is seated, [8] . ► Burrowing pressive pains in the sound back teeth of the right side, in the evening, [34] . ► Jerking-sticking pain in the left upper teeth (sixth day), [20] . ► Violent sticking and throbbing in two carious teeth (third day), [29] . ► A stitch in a tooth, and extending from it upward into the whole left side of the face; a drawing and jerking pain rhythmical with the pulse, with jerklike twinges into the ear, and painfulness in the right eye on moving it, [8] . ► Jerking stitches in a tooth which extend to the ear, across the cheek-bone, as a drawing-jerking in the ear, which pains like the earache, and extending to the eye, which becomes very much affected and painful, so that he can endure neither motion nor light, and the eyeball becomes painful to touch, [8] . ► Sudden drawing jerking-tearing and sticking in a left lower sound incisor, which soon extends through the whole jaw, backward and upward, relieved by cold water in the mouth, at 7 P.M. (fourth day), [13] . ► Jerking pain in a right upper molar, which had been hollow for three years, recurring after six minutes, and disappearing after rinsing the mouth with cold water (third morning), [11] . ► Tearing-jerking toothache in the whole row of the upper jaw (first day), [14] . ► Gums. ► The gum of a left lower back tooth is painful as if sore, worse while eating, [4] . [210.] ► Sore pain in the gum, increased by chewing hard food, [30] . ► Gum and teeth somewhat sensitive on chewing (sixth day), [30] . ► Tearing-jerking pain in the place where a left upper wisdom tooth had been drawn seven years previously; this pain extended to the zygoma and the temporal region, not relieved by cold water taken into the mouth; waking him from sleep at 11 P.M., and lasting till nearly 2 o'clock, when he was able to sleep for two hours, after which it continued but less violent and of shorter duration (second night), [12] . ► Tongue. ► Coated tongue (sixth day), [26] . ► Tongue coated (after 300 drops), [20] . ► Tongue thickly coated on waking, [22] . ► Tongue coated white (third and seventh day), [30] . ► Tongue dry (after three-quarters of an hour), [15] . ► Tongue dry in the morning on waking, [8] . ► Tongue on waking was mostly dry and rough, [30] . [220.] ► Sensation of dryness on the tongue, with roughness and acidity, as after rinsing the mouth with an astringent (fourth day), [30] . ► A dull boring stitch at the root of the tongue, [4] . ► General Mouth. ► Aphthæ in the mouth, [31] . ► Cracks across the right arch of the palate and on the right margin of the tongue, extremely painful (thirteenth day); the next day, the cracks in the tongue were surrounded by hard margins, and were extremely pain, [32] . ► Mouth and tongue very dry (third day), [29] . ► Sensation of coldness in the mouth, aggravated by inspiring cold air through it, soon after taking the drug (first day), [30] . ► Tension in the soft palate, and discharge of compact mucus (apparently the remains of a cold that had just passed off), [22] . ► Slight sticking pain in the right arch of the palate; which decidedly impeded the motion of the tongue (thirteenth day), [32] . ► Saliva. ► Much saliva in the mouth, [28] . ► The expectorated saliva is mixed with blood, [4] . ► Taste. [230.] ► Taste pasty and flat (seventh day), [30] . ► An unpleasant pasty taste in the mouth (fourth day), [29] . ► A sour taste on the tip of the tongue (first day), [20] . ► Sensation as though the tip of the tongue were constantly acid, if it touched the front teeth; with somewhat increased watery saliva; this lasted four days, and was most noticed with an empty stomach, and immediately after taking the drug (20th dil.), [19] . ► Bitter taste in the mouth, [28] . ► Speech. ► Speech very weak (third day), [32] . THROAT. ► Dryness of the throat, [21] . ► Burning, redness, vesicles and ulcers in the throat (from chewing the fresh leaves), [36] . ► Constant stitching in the throat, especially on swallowing, [35] . ► Rawness in the throat, [22] . [240.] ► Rawness in the throat, with hoarse voice, constant spitting of watery saliva (second day), [20] . ► Uvula. ► Sensation as though the uvula were too long (third day), [29] . ► Some sensitiveness of the uvula continues (fourth day), [29] . ► External Throat. ► Swelling of the submaxillary glands, [31] . ► Swelling of the submaxillary glands in the right triangle in the right side of the neck became more and more painful, motion of the head in any direction was more difficult; the gland on the left side seemed slightly enlarged and painful to touch; secretion of saliva was very profuse (sixth day), [17] . ► Slight swelling of the submaxillary glands, which increased for eight days, and then gradually decreased (after eight days), [15] . ► The submaxillary glands are swollen, with some lumps, and feel tense as if they would suppurate; they are painful to touch, and cause pains in the teeth, [8] . ► Slight swelling in the right upper triangle of the neck, just under the lower jaw, with increased secretion of saliva (fifth day), [17] . ► Great swelling of the thyroid gland (fourth day), [32] . STOMACH. ► Appetite. ► Appetite increased, [21] ; (first day), [13] ; (second day), [11] . [250.] ► Appetite increased; the prover ate double his usual amount, with haste (third day), [32] . ► Appetite rather increased, [25] . ► Constantly increasing appetite for some days after the proving, [16] . ► Appetite exceptionally good (first day), [32] . ► The more violent the attack was at the time of eating the greater the appetite seemed to be (eighth day), [32] . ► Great hunger, towards evening (third day), [11] . ► Appetite unnaturally great (twelfth day), [32] . ► Ravenous appetite towards 2 P.M., followed by discomfort after eating (second day), [29] . ► More appetite for dinner than usual (second day), [16] . ► Diminished appetite (second day), [13] . [260.] ► Little appetite (sixth day), [27] . ► Very little appetite (fourth day), [24] . ► Complete loss of appetite, [28] ; (third day), [29] . ► No desire for food and speedy satiety, [30] . ► No appetite at noon, [22] ; (first day), [14] . ► No appetite at noon, on account of weakness, [22] . ► No desire for breakfast, [22] ; (second day), [29] . ► Very little appetite for supper (first night), [29] . ► Does not relish tobacco (sixth day), [20] . ► Does not relish the accustomed cigar (seventh day), [30] . [270.] ► Long-enduring satiety; though he can eat much dinner and relishes the food, yet he immediately feels that he has eaten too much, and will never need food any more, [9] . ► Thirst. ► Thirst, [21] . ► Thirst, with longing for ice, which does not refresh him (third day), [29] . ► Thirst increased, [16] ; (after three-quarters of an hour), [15] . ► Thirst increased in the afternoon (second day), [30] . ► Increased thirst, in the evening, [22] . ► Eructations. ► Eructations and nausea, [32] . ► Frequent eructations for several minutes (first day), [26] . ► Three eructations, immediately, [8] . ► Empty, tasteless eructations, [28] . [280.] ► Eructations of air (after 300 drops), [20] . ► Nausea and Vomiting. ► Nausea, [35] . ► Nausea, without vomiting, [31] . ► Nausea and general sick feeling, [30b] . ► Nausea, with inclination to vomit, towards 11 A.M., [14] . ► Nausea while smoking, though the prover was a great smoker, [30] . ► Nausea after eating, while smoking, [6] . ► Nausea after smoking that causes a sensation of weakness in the lower limbs, so that they seem distorted, and he is obliged to lie down, [2] . ► Smoking cigars, to which he was accustomed, caused nausea and vomiting (eighth and ninth days), [16] . ► Sensation of nausea soon (one hour after 40 drops), (tenth day), [30] . [290.] ► Inclination to vomit, [28] . ► Stomach. ► Distension of the stomach and abdomen (tenth day), [26] . ► Faintness in the stomach, with yawning, [22] . ► Sensation of heaviness in the pit of the stomach (first day), [13] . ► Tensive sensation in the epigastric region (first day), [26] . ► Constriction in the stomach, and after a short time also in the abdomen, which was tense and hard to the touch, followed by vomiting, with great exertion, of a bitter fluid, more watery than slimy, in which there were some streaks of pale red blood, [21] . ► Pressure in the stomach (seventh day), [30] . ► Pressure on stomach, after dinner (first day), [33] . ► Pressure in the epigastric region, with tension over the whole abdomen (third day), [26] . ► Slight pressure in the stomach (fourth day), [30] . [300.] ► Pressive pain in stomach, after dinner (first day), [33] . ► Slight pressive pain in epigastric region, after dinner (third day), [33] . ► Oppression in the stomach (third day), [32] . ABDOMEN. ► Hypochondria. ► Pains alternately in the region of the liver and spleen, but only on certain motions, and soon disappearing, without sensitiveness of these regions (third day), [29] . ► Painful sensation in the region of the liver, aggravated by pressure, [30] . ► Tension and sticking in both hypochondria, impeding respiration (seventh day), [30] . ► Transient sticking in the left hypochondrium (fifth day), [32] . ► The sticking pain in the left hypochondrium became definitely localized in the spleen, with a dull tensive sensation in the fundus of the stomach, aggravated by pressure (fifth day), [32] . ► Sticking pain in the hepatic region from time to time, especially on certain motions and positions of the body (second day), [29] . ► Stitches at times in the hepatic and splenic regions (third day), [30] . [310.] ► Numerous transient stitches in both hypochondria (sixth day), [30] . ► Frequent transient stitches in the liver and spleen, [30] . ► The region of the liver is painful to touch and to stooping, as if bruised, lasting two weeks, [8] . ► General Abdomen. ► Distension of the abdomen, with rumbling (first day), [13] . ► Very great collection of air in the abdomen, during the first hours, [35] . ► Rumbling in the abdomen, as if it were empty (after one hour), [6] . ► Rumbling in the abdomen, which extends into a hernia (third day), [12] . ► Rumbling in the abdomen, for two hours in the afternoon (second day), [20] . ► Passage of much flatus, [22] . ► Fulness of abdomen, afternoons (first day), [33] . [320.] ► Fulness and tension in abdomen (first day), [33] . ► Sensation of fulness and tension in the whole abdomen (in general free from flatulence), as after a full meal, between 4 and 5 P.M., increased until 9 P.M., when he went to bed, [11] . ► Sensation of distension and tension in the abdomen, a painful tense sensation in the whole parietes of the abdomen, worse from touch (second day), [32] . ► Sensitive tension and pulling in each abdominal muscle on attempting to move about the room; it is very painful when coughing (second day), [32] . ► Cutting contractive pain in the abdomen, [21] . ► Griping in the abdomen, with rolling and rumbling for half an hour (seventh day), [20] . ► Some griping in the abdomen (sixth day), [20] . ► Drawing pain in the abdomen, especially in the umbilical region, aggravated by pressure, [35] . ► A contractive cutting pain in the right abdominal and kidney regions, [2] . ► Frequent cutting colic, [35] . [330.] ► While urinating, sticking from the abdomen up to the chest, aggravated by inspiration (after fifteen hours), [4] . ► Hypogastrium and Iliac Regions. ► Painfulness of the lower abdominal region, extending into the penis (third day), [29] . ► Violent colic in the hypogastrium, followed by some diarrha (eighth day), [30] . ► *Sensation in the abdominal ring and in the inguinal glands, as if a swelling would form, [30] . ► Pressing-out pain in the right abdominal ring, as if a hernia would protrude, or as though something had protruded, [1] . ► *Some swelling of the right inguinal region, painful to touch (second day), [29] . ► Pain in the inner and outer inguinal region, only on the left side (sixth day), [30] . ► Tension in the left inguinal region, which extended down into the thighs, and was worse while walking (second day), [30] . ► *While taking a long walk, an uncomfortable tension in the inguinal region, with swelling of both inguinal glands , subsequently the left inguinal gland became more swollen than the right; the swelling disappeared after a week, [27] . ► *Increased sensitiveness of both inguinal regions, although the swelling of the right inguinal gland (noticed on the first day) had almost entirely disappeared (third day), [29] . [340.] ► Dragging towards the right inguinal canal, with frequent protrusion of the hernia (which he had always had), (third day), [11] . ► Transient burning stitches in the left inguinal ring now and then; the prover involuntarily grasped the spot, and was astonished to find that the glands were harder and larger than those of the right side (fifth day), [23] . [Swelling in the inguinal glands (bubo)], [10] . [In a syphilitic subject. -Hughes.] ►► *Swelling of both inguinal glands , with sensitiveness while walking; the right inguinal glands become more swollen, with prickling pain in them, aggravated by walking, and worse in bed; afterwards with a tensive sensation in them, especially while walking (swelling of the glands commenced on the sixth day and continued more than two weeks), [26] . ►► *Swelling of the right inguinal glands , worse than the left, not painful, when rising after waking (sixth day), [17] . ► *Great pain in the right inguinal glands (fifth day), [26] . ► Tension and pain in the inguinal glands and inguinal rings (seventh day), [30] . ► Tension in the left inguinal glands (sixth day), [30] . ► Jerking pain in the inguinal glands, [1] . ► Unpleasant prickling sensation in the right inguinal glands, lasting ten minutes (fourth day), [26] . RECTUM AND ANUS. ► Rectum. [350.] ► Sudden sensation of warmth in the rectum, followed after a few minutes by urging to stool (after four hours), [32] . ► Itching in the rectum and genitals, [22] . ► Burning in the anus and swelling of the hæmorrhoids, during two stools, [22] . ► Burning and itching in the anus, after an easy stool, [22] . ► Itching in the anus (third morning), [12] . ► The hæmorrhoids were swollen and covered with slimy mucus (third morning), [12] . ► Violent itching in the hæmorrhoids, and slight discharge of mucus from them (third evening), [12] . ► Urging. ► Frequent urging to stool, without effect (fifth day), [17] . ► Violent urging to stool, without result (after nine hours), [32] . ► Violent urging to stool, immediately after eating, stool profuse and thin, with painful burning in the anus, repeated after half an hour (third day), [32] . [360.] ► Urgent desire for stool, which was pasty and accompanied by much flatulence (third morning), [12] . STOOL. ► Diarrha. ► Diarrha-like stools (third day), [29] . ► Two somewhat diarrhic stools during the second forenoon, [29] . ► Frequent stool, which always became thinner and thinner, without colic (after thirty-three days), [4] . ► Stools increased and pasty, [30] . ► Two pasty evacuations (second day), [23] . ► Pasty stool, with burning in the rectum, which latter occurred several times during the day, [22] . ► Very copious, thin, pasty stool (first day), [33] . ► Three liquid stools in the course of the forenoon, [21] . [It is doubtful whether these were the result of Clematis, since the prover often suffered from pains in the abdomen, usually with liquid stools, before menstruation (which came on that night).] ► Stool easy, with sensation of heat, followed by burning in the rectum, [22] . [370.] ► Some blood with the stool, [22] . ► Constipation. ► *Continued constipation , for several days after the proving, [16] . ► The stool continued constipated until the seventeenth day, after which he had two or three stools a day (very seldom one), without urging, [16] . ► Stool became at first less frequent and harder, [31] . ► Hard stool, after much urging (fourth evening), [16] . ► No stool, contrary to custom (third day), [16] . URINARY ORGANS. ► Kidneys and Bladder. ► The pain, which had hitherto involved the spleen, stomach, and heart, continued in an aggravated form, and involved the left kidney (eighth day), [32] . ► Stitches in the bladder, [35] . ► Severe transient stitches in the bladder, [35] . ► Urethra. ► Slight mucous discharge from the urethra (thirteenth day), [32] . [380.] ► Transient sensation of coldness in the urethra, [22] . ► *Burning in the urethra, on urinating (first day), [33] . ► Burning pain in the orifice of the urethra, when passing the last drops of urine (third day), [29] . ► Slight burning in the navicular fossa, on urinating (third morning), [12] . ► On urinating, slight burning in urethra, which is painful to touch (first day), [33] . ► *When beginning to urinate, it burns (bites) the worst, while urinating it sticks in the urethra, and after urinating it still continues to burn and bite ; when not urinating there is an irritation in the forepart of the penis, [1] . ► *Long-continued contraction and constriction in the urethra ; the urine could only pass by drops, as in spasmodic stricture, [1] . ► Stitches in the urethra, [35] . ► Transient stitches in the urethra (tenth day), [32] . ► Stitches in the orifice of the urethra, [35] . [390.] ► Stitches, as with needles, in the orifice of the urethra, [35] . ► At evening, twinging pains in the urethra, several times (second, third, and fourth days), [33] . ► In the evening, twinging pains in urethra when not urinating, on standing, relieved on sitting, disappearing on lying in bed at night (third day), [33] . ► The urethra is painful to touch, [1] . ► Itching-tickling for several minutes after urinating, returning after a pause of half an hour, and renewed after every micturition (twelfth day), [32] . ► Frequent tickling in the urethra, especially in the navicular fossa, [25] . ► Itching in the navicular fossa of the urethra (seventh day), [27] . ► Dull transient pain in the prostate, frequently repeated through the day (tenth day), [32] . ► Urging to urinate, [35] . ► Urging to urinate, without pain, [6] . [400.] ► (Frequent urging to urinate), [35] ; (seventh day), [27] . ► Frequent urging to rise and urinate, followed by some burning at the orifice of the urethra (seventh night), [32] . ► Frequent urging to urinate, with slight discharge; urine clear, reddish; on urinating, an unpleasant pressing sensation in the genitals, also when not urinating, [34] . ► Micturition. ► Frequent evacuation of normal urine (third day), [13] . ► Frequent micturition followed by prickling-burning in the orifice of the urethra (tenth day), [32] . ► Frequent urinating at night, [34] . ► Increased passage of urine, [21] . ► Profuse urine, [10] ; (first day), [23] . ► Urine copious and turbid, [21] . ► Urine scanty (third day), [29] . [410.] ► Urine scanty and dark-red (seventh morning), [16] . ► Urine scanty, thickish, of a peculiar smell, [22] . ► Urine scanty, saturated, of offensive odor, [22] . ► Secretion of urine at first increased, but afterwards diminished, [23] . ► *Micturition was remarkably slow , and with a very thin stream (seventh day), [32] . ► *Urine passed slowly and in a thin stream, as if the urethra were constricted ; its passage was followed by burning in the urethra, and a tickling, itching, and sticking in its orifice (eleventh day), [32] . ► He is unable to evacuate all the urine at once ; it frequently stops while running; after a time (if he makes a great exertion) another portion comes, then again it stops, and so stops several times until it is all evacuated; then several drops involuntarily of it, lasting a minute; in the internal of the stoppage of the urine he felt a jerk-like tearing in the forepart of the urethra (after nine hours), [9] . ► Urine. ► Urine red, [30] . ► Urine dark red, without sediment (second day), [29] . ► Urine reddish, profuse without pain (after three to six hours), [9] . [420.] ► Urine dark yellow (third morning), [12] . ► Urine smells like Russia leather, [22] . ► Urine hot (seventh day), [30] . ► [He urinates a purulent substance], [10] [In a case of gonorrhal rheumatism; as this appeared the arthritis disappeared. -Hughes.] ► Urine turbid, milky, streaked with floating flocks of mucus, with very thick froth (eleventh day), [32] . ► Urine saturated, smelling of violets, hot, [22] . ► Urine deposits a large quantity of crystals of "urate of soda" (ninth day), [30] . >>>>> Copyright © Médi-T ® 2000 Main

keynotes.text: A Text-Book of Materia Medica. (Characteristic, Analytical, and Comparative.) By Pr. Allen Corson Cowperthwaite, M. D., PH. D., LL. D. CLEMATIS. (Clematis Erecta.) Natural order.- Ranunculaceae. Common name.- Upright Virgin's Bower. General Analysis.- Acts especially upon the skin, the lymphatic glandular system, and the genito-urinary organs. Characteristic symptoms. Head.  (Clematis erecta) Eruption on occiput (Lyc., Petrol., Sep.), extending down the neck, moist sore, with crawling and stinging, itching ; often drying up in scales ; itching worse when getting warm in bed (Merc.) ; only slight temporary relief from scratching. Ears.- (Clematis erecta) Burning pains in the auricles, with heat. Eyes.- (Clematis erecta) Inflammation of the eyes ; smarting and rawness. Mouth.- (Clematis erecta) Toothache, worse on assuming the horizontal position ; better for a short time from cold water (Bism., Coff.) or drawing in the air ; worse from warmth of bed ( Med ., from crumb of bread.) Abdomen.- (Clematis erecta) Increased sensitiveness of both inguinal regions. Swelling and induration of the inguinal glands ( Calc. c., Nitr. ac.) ; with pricking pains. Stool.- (Clematis erecta) Constipation. (Alum ., Bry., Calc. c ., Nux v., Op .) Urinary Organs.- (Clematis erecta) Interrupted flow of urine ( Coni .), with burning during, but most at the beginning of micturition, or during the interruptions. Long-lasting contraction and constriction of the urethra. Unable to evacuate all the urine at once. Male Organs.- (Clematis erecta) Swelling of right half of scrotum ( Puls .) ; testicles relaxed, hanging down. (Camph., Sulph.) Painful, inflamed, and swollen testicles.(Rhod., Spong.) Pain in testicles drawing to spermatic cord.(Spong.) Right spermatic cord sensitive, testicle drawn up. (Rhod.) Skin.- (Clematis erecta) Painful swelling and induration of glands. (Calc. c., Iodi.) Moist, itching eczema (Petrol., Graph ., Merc.) ; worse from washing in cold water ; from warmth of bed (Alum., Merc .) ; from wet poultices. Therapeutic Range.- (Clematis erecta) Scrofulous and mercurial affections ; eczema ; crusta lactea ; diseases of the glands ; stricture of the urethra ; ulcers ; orchitis ; epididymitis. Compare.- (Clematis erecta) Ars., Bry., Calc. c., Coni., Graph., Merc., Petrol., Puls., Rhus tox., Sil., Sulph. Antidotes.- (Clematis erecta) Bry ., for the toothache. Camph. Copyright © Robert Séror 2003

indications.text: The fresh stems, leaves, and blossoms of Clematis virginiana, Linné. Nat. Ord.—Ranunculaceae. COMMON NAMES: Virgin's bower, Ladies' bower, Love vine, Traveler's ivy. ILLUSTRATION: Lloyd's Drugs and Med. of N. A., Vol. I, Pl. 1.

Botanical Source.—Clematis virginiana is a perennial, climbing plant, with a stem from 8 to 15 feet or more in length, supporting itself on shrubs, fences and brushwood, by means of its long petioles. The leaves are opposite, deep-green, and ternate; the leaflets ovate, cordate, acuminate, lobed, cut-dentate, from 2 to 3 inches in length by 1 or 2 in breadth. The flowers are in paniculate clusters, and dioecious; the panicles being large, axillary, and dichotomous. Sepals 4, white, spreading, oval-oblong, obtuse-petaloid. Stamens from 28 to 36. The fruit is furnished with long, plumose tails, appearing in large, downy tufts. The seeds are compressed (W.).

History.—The Clematis virginiana is a native of the United States, and grows by river banks, in hedges and thickets, from Canada to Georgia and the Mississippi. It flowers in July and August; the parts used are the bark, leaves, and blossoms, which yield their virtues to water or alcohol. The leaves should be gathered when they are fully grown, say in August. The fresh drug only should be employed, as most of its properties are dissipated in drying. Clematis is not found as a drug in commerce. Alcohol takes up the properties of clematis, yielding a green tincture, which, upon exposure to light, turns brown. This and all of the below-mentioned species of Clematis have been used in medicine to some extent, but only this species and Clematis recta are now employed, and chiefly in domestic practice and by Homoeopathic physicians. Though never favorite remedies, they have probably fallen into undeserved neglect on account of having been used in the dried instead of the fresh state. They should be given a proper trial, and their worth or worthlessness established.

Description.—The leaves and flowers are described above. "The stem attains a diameter at the base from ½ inch to 1 inch, and has a spongy, ligneous texture. When recent, it is covered with a thin brown bark. The wood is coarsely divided into distinct medullary rays, between which, when the plant is recent, are deposited layers of a greenish substance, which contains the acrid principles." (J. U. Lloyd, in Drugs and Med. of N. A., Vol. I, p. 7).

Chemical Composition.—According to Rafinesque (1830) a peculiar body resembling gluten, and known as clematin, exists in the flowers of C. virginiana and C. Viorna. The fresh plant (Clematis virginiana), according to Prof. J. U. Lloyd, who examined it chemically, has a peculiar, unpleasant odor, and a taste at first rank and disagreeable, but, after prolonged chewing, becomes acrid and irritating, not followed by pain, but rather leaving "a dry, metallic-like roughness of the tongue and mouth." When distilled with water a neutral distillate, having an odor recalling that of skunk-cabbage, was obtained. This odor may be removed by agitation with chloroform or benzol. If this solution be spontaneously evaporated, "a colorless, oily substance remains, which is the characteristic principle of the plant, but which evaporates by exposure." He found the distillate, when inhaled, to be a pulmonary irritant, giving a sensation similar to that produced by sulphurous acid gas. No alkaloid, either volatile or fixed, was found. Besides the usual plant constituents the plant contains grape-sugar. (See paper by Prof. J. U. Lloyd, in Drugs and Med. of N. A., Vol. I, p. 10).

Action, Medical Uses, and Dosage.—The various species of clematis, when applied to the skin in a fresh state, blister it; and if taken internally, act as corrosive poisons. Both drying and boiling destroy the virulent property. They have been used externally in the treatment of several cutaneous affections, and in form of a liniment, made with oil, for the cure of itch; internally, as diuretics and sudorifics in chronic rheumatism, palsy, etc., in minute doses. The extract, in doses of 1 or 2 grains, is recommended for osteocopic pains. The green leaves bruised are sometimes employed to produce vesication, also, as an escharotic and detergent for venereal and other foul and indolent u1cers.

Clematis virginiana has been highly spoken of as a nervine in uterine diseases. Place 2 drachms of the dried leaf into a cup filled with hot water, cover it, and allow it to stand until the liquid is cool enough to drink; strain, sweeten with sugar if desired, and let the patient drink it at once. Repeat as often as may be required, the doses being regulated by its effects upon the system.

Like Clematis recta (which see), this species produces painful eczema-like eruptions, which may result in small painful ulcerations. Prof. E. M. Hale, M.D., has found it fully equal to Clematis recta, being particularly useful in nervous insomnia, neuralgic and rheumatic headache, toothache, reflex neuroses of women from ovarian or urinary irritation, neuroses of men with pain in testicles and bladder, cystitis, urethritis, gonorrhoea, orchitis, and swellings of the inguinal glands. Following the law of similia, he also found it useful in "eczema, herpes zoster, and pustular eruptions on the scalp and face of children." A good tincture may be prepared as follows: Clematis (fresh stem, leaves, and flowers), 1 part; alcohol, 2 parts; bruise to an even pulp, add the alcohol, mix thoroughly, and allow to macerate in a close vessel for 10 days. Express and filter (D. and M. of N. A.). Ɣ As clematis acts very much after the manner of pulsatilla and its congeners, it should be tried in fractional doses in the complaints for which such drugs have an established reputation. (For uses of other species see Related Species). Dose, 1/10 to 5 drops, well diluted.

Related Species.—Prof. Landerer, of Greece (1877), reported a case of epilepsy cured after futile attempts with other medicine, by a species of Clematis, either C. cirrhosa or C. sylvestris. Also rubefacient and vesicant.

Clematis recta (erecta), Linné. Upright Virgin's bower.—The Flammula Jovis of old medical writers and the first species introduced as a medicine (D. and M. of N. A.). This species, like others of its family, will produce a painful pustular, eczema-like eruption, which may result in blebs or bullae, and even develop into small painful ulcers. It has been used to some extent in Homoeopathy, and is mentioned by Prof. Webster (Dynam. Therap.) in the conditions named below. It appears to affect both the male and female reproductive organs, influencing both the testes and ovaries. Ovarian indurations and chronic gonorrhoeal orchitis are said to be relieved by it. It is also reputed useful in other after-effects of gonorrhoea, as gleet and incipient stricture. It relieves irritation of the urinary tract, especially the vesical irritation of nervous women with ovarian derangements. Dysuria and urinal retention are also occasionally benefited by it. Homoeopathists employ it in chronic scrofulous and syphilitic skin diseases, especially when mercurialization has been carried too far. Foul, vesicular, and pustular eruptions, ulcers, syphilitic excrescences, eczema, and irritated and swollen eyelids, involving the meibomian glands, are conditions in which they claim success from its use. A Homoeopathic tincture may be used in the proportion of 10 to 15 drops to 4 fluid ounces of water, the dose of which is a teaspoonful several times a day. Störck (1769) employed Clematis recta in old ulcers, secondary syphilis headache, and carcinoma. He also pointed out its diuretic action, a view confirmed by Sauveur, in 1866, who claims to have cured Bright's disease with its infusion.

Clematis Vitalba, Linné.—The common species of Europe and only one in England. "Virgin's bower, Traveler's joy, Love vine, White vine, Ladies' bower, Old Man's beard, Smoke wood, Wild vine, Bind-with, Hedge vine, and Climbers" (D. and M. of N. A.), Wild clematis. Gaube extracted a principle from this plant to which he gave the name clematine. It is alkaline, and forms a neutral compound with sulphuric acid, which crystallizes in hexagonal needles. Besides this principle, he also detected an essential oil, to which it owes it properties, tannin, mucilaginous substances, and a small amount of earthy salts.

The seeds of clematis, given in infusion, have been found serviceable in albuminuria, even when general anasarca, amblyopia, incipient hypertrophy of the left ventricle, without valvular lesion, and which condition is, as M. Traube has shown, always a result of abnormal conditions of the kidneys, and other symptoms peculiar to this disease were present. The effects of the remedy were quite prompt, a profuse diuresis, followed by a gradual diminution of albumin in the urine, and a rapid disappearance of the anasarca, and other symptoms. This infusion has likewise proved efficient in other serous affections due to other maladies of the abdominal viscera (Prof. Sauveur, 1886). The roots of the C. Vitalba, boiled for a short time to diminish their acrimony, and then infused in boiling oil, were applied to the skin several times a day, in itch, and a cure was effected in 12 or 15 applications. The plant, boiled in oil and mixed with wax and verdigris, was formerly esteemed a remedy for tinea.

Clematis Viorna, Linné, or Leather flower, which is more common in the western states, and may be found growing in woods from Pennsylvania southward, may, probably, be employed as a substitute for the above. It differs from it in having a cylindrical, striate stem; with opposite, decompound, pinnately divided leaves, consisting of from 9 to 12 ovate-lanceolate leaflets, acute at each end, entire or 3-lobed; flowers large, purple, nodding, solitary, axillary, campanulate; sepals thick, leathery, acuminate, and peduncles from 3 to 6 inches long, with a pair of small, simple, entire leaves near the middle (W.).

Clematis Pitcheri, Torrey and Gray.—From Illinois westward. This and above species are probably varieties of the same species. It has single, dull-purple flowers, having thick, leathery, valvate sepals. It is also known as Leather flower.

Clematis crispa, Linné.—Southern United States. This is thought to be the most acrid of the indigenous species. It is known in the south as Blue jasmine and Curled Virgin's bower. It has single nodding flowers, with "purplish-blue sepals, with dilated thin margins" (D. and M. of N. A.).
Clematis Flammula, Sweet-scented Virgin's bower.—France and other parts of south Europe. Has fragrant white flowers and is cultivated for ornamentation (D. and M. of N. A.).
Clematis Viticella, Linné; Blue clematis.—South Europe, particularly France. Cultivated, and has blue flowers (D. and M. of N. A.). Formerly esteemed in itch and leprosy.

Clematis verticillaris, De Candolle; Whorl-leaved Virgin's bower.—Rare northern species. "It has large, 4-sepaled, purple flowers, with thin, spreading sepals" (D. and M. of N. A.). Cultivated for ornament.
Clematis alpina, Miller.—Southern Europe, in mountainous regions. Analogous to preceding (D. and M. of N. A.).
Clematis ligusticifolia, Nuttall; Wild sarsaparilla.—Western United States. Closely resembles Clematis virginiana. Root used by New Mexico Indians as an alterative (D. and M. of N. A.)

Clematis dioica, Linné.—Jamaica. Used as a rubefacient (D. and M. of N. A.). The root, boiled with sea water, acts as a powerful hydragogue cathartic, and is useful in dropsy; and an infusion of the leaves and flowers removes spots and freckles from the skin.

Clematis mauritiana, Linné.—Madagascar. "Probably the most acrid of all the genus" (D. and M. of N. A.). Employed by the negroes of the Isle of France to blister the cheek for the relief of toothache.
King's American Dispensatory, 1898, was written by Harvey Wickes Felter, M.D., and John Uri Lloyd, Phr. M., Ph. D.
